solution: 
```cpp
#include <string>

using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    int minStartingIndex(string s, string pattern) {
        int n = s.size();
        int m = pattern.size();
        for (int i = 0; i <= n - m; ++i) {
            int mismatches = 0;
            for (int j = 0; j < m; ++j) {
                if (s[i + j] != pattern[j]) {
                    ++mismatches;
                    if (mismatches > 1) {
                        break;
                    }
                }
            }
            if (mismatches <= 1) {
                return i;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
};
```

efficiency: MEDIUM

time complexity: O((n - m + 1) * m)

space complexity: O(1)

energy implications: MEDIUM

explanation: The solution uses a sliding window approach to check each possible substring of 's' that has the same length as the pattern. For each window, it counts the number of mismatches and returns the first window with at most one mismatch. This approach ensures we find the smallest starting index efficiently, though it may not handle the largest inputs optimally due to its O(n*m) complexity.
